paraphrase_2: NLP applications often demand the detection of word meaning relationships that extend past synonymy and paraphrasing.
paraphrase_3: Identifying relations between word meanings that go beyond synonymy and paraphrasing is essential for many NLP applications.

originals: For instance, given “Carlsen plays chess.” and the question “Which game does Carlsen play?”, successfully answering the question requires knowing that chess is a kind of game, or more generally, that chess entails game.

While prior work has defined lexical entailment as a relation between word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we argue entailment relations are better defined when illustrating word meaning with an example in context.
paraphrase_1: Although previous research has characterized lexical entailment as a relationship among word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we contend that entailment relations achieve better definition through contextual examples that demonstrate word meaning.
paraphrase_2: Previous studies have established lexical entailment as a connection between word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), yet we maintain that contextual examples illustrating word meaning provide superior definitions for entailment relations.
paraphrase_3: Lexical entailment has been conceptualized in earlier work as a word type relation (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), but we propose that entailment relations receive more effective definition when word meaning is demonstrated through contextual examples.
paraphrase_4: Though earlier research framed lexical entailment as existing between word types (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we assert that entailment relations benefit from better definition when contextual examples showcase word meaning.

originals: Ignoring context is problematic since entailment might hold between some senses of the words, but not others.
paraphrase_1: The problem with disregarding context is that entailment may be valid for certain word senses while invalid for others.
paraphrase_2: Context cannot be overlooked because entailment could exist between particular meanings of the words but be absent in different meanings.
paraphrase_3: Since entailment may apply to specific word senses but not to alternative ones, dismissing context creates difficulties.
paraphrase_4: It is problematic to ignore context given that entailment relationships might exist for some interpretations of the words while failing for others.

In this paper, we investigate how to represent and compare the meaning of words in context for lexical entailment.
paraphrase_1: This paper explores methods for representing and comparing contextual word meanings in the context of lexical entailment.
paraphrase_2: We examine approaches to represent and evaluate the semantic content of words within their contexts for lexical entailment purposes.
paraphrase_3: The focus of our research is on developing techniques to capture and contrast word meanings as they appear in context for lexical entailment tasks.
paraphrase_4: Our study addresses the challenge of how contextual word meanings can be represented and compared when dealing with lexical entailment.

originals: Since distributional representations for word types have proved useful to detect lexical entailment out of context in supervised settings (Baroni et al., 2012; Roller et al., 2014; Turney and Mohammad, 2013), we propose to transform context-agnostic word type representations into contextualized representations that highlight salient properties of the context (Section 3), and use these contextualized representations with a range of semantic similarity features (Section 4) to successfully detect entailment.

As we will see, these context representations significantly improve performance over contextagnostic baselines not only in English, but also between English and French words (Section 7) on two novel datasets (Section 5).
We also show that our features are sensitive to word sense changes indicated by context, and adequately capture the direction of entailment relation (Section 8).
paraphrase_1: Section 8 demonstrates that our features respond appropriately to contextual shifts in word meaning and effectively encode the directional nature of entailment relationships.
paraphrase_2: Additionally, we demonstrate that contextual variations in word sense are detected by our features, which also properly represent the orientation of entailment connections (Section 8).
paraphrase_3: Our findings reveal that the features we developed are responsive to context-driven word sense variations and successfully model the directional aspects of entailment relations (Section 8).
paraphrase_4: Furthermore, evidence indicates that our proposed features detect word sense shifts based on contextual cues while appropriately modeling entailment direction (Section 8).

originals: Moreover, we establish a new state-of-the-art on an existing dataset that captures a broader range of semantic relations in context (Shwartz and Dagan, 2015), and show that the proposed features, induced solely from large amounts of raw text, yield systems that perform as well, or better than existing systems that require additional human annotation.

We say that entailment holds if the meaning of wl in the context of cl is more specific than the meaning of wr in the context of cr.

paraphrase_3: The condition for entailment is that wl interpreted in the context of cl has greater specificity of meaning than wr interpreted in the context of cr.
paraphrase_4: Entailment exists when the contextual meaning of wl in cl demonstrates more specificity than the contextual meaning of wr in cr.

However, we limit entailment to the specificity relation in this work to better understand the impact of context.

originals: Note that lexical entailment in context is not textual entailment.

originals: Recognizing textual entailment (Dagan et al., 2013) and natural language inference (Bowman et al., 2015) involves detecting entailment relations between sentences, while lexical entailment is a relation between words.

originals: Following Thater et al.

(2011); Erk and Padó (2008), our first approach is to apply a filter to word type representations to highlight the salient dimensions of the exemplar context, emphasizing relevant dimensions of and downplaying unimportant ones.

However, while prior work represents context by averaging word vectors, we propose richer representations that better capture the salient geometrical properties of the exemplar context that might get lost by averaging (Figure 1).
paraphrase_1: Although previous research has represented context through word vector averaging, our approach introduces more sophisticated representations that effectively preserve the important geometrical characteristics of exemplar context which averaging might obscure (Figure 1).
paraphrase_2: Prior studies have relied on averaging word vectors to represent context, but we suggest enhanced representations that more successfully retain the crucial geometrical features of exemplar context that could be diminished through averaging (Figure 1).
paraphrase_3: While earlier work has employed word vector averaging for context representation, we put forward enriched representations that superior capture the significant geometrical attributes of exemplar context potentially lost during averaging (Figure 1).
paraphrase_4: Previous approaches have used averaging of word vectors as their method for context representation, yet we advocate for more nuanced representations that better preserve the essential geometrical properties of exemplar context that averaging may eliminate (Figure 1).

originals: First, we construct fixed length representations for the contexts cl and cr by running convolutional filters over Cl and Cr.
paraphrase_1: To begin, we create fixed-length representations for contexts cl and cr through the application of convolutional filters across Cl and Cr.
paraphrase_2: Initially, fixed-length representations of contexts cl and cr are built by applying convolutional filters to Cl and Cr.
paraphrase_3: The process starts with generating fixed-length representations for contexts cl and cr via convolutional filter operations on Cl and Cr.
paraphrase_4: We begin by producing fixed-length representations of the contexts cl and cr using convolutional filters that process Cl and Cr.

originals: Specifically, we calculate the column-wise maximum, minimum and the mean over the matrices Cl and Cr, as done by (Tang et al., 2014) for supervised sentiment classification.
This yields three ddimensional vectors for cl (~cl,max, ~cl,min, ~cl,mean), and three d-dimensional vectors for cr (~cr,max, ~cr,min, ~cr,mean).

Computing the maximum and minimum across all vector dimensions captures the exterior surface of the “instance manifold”
3
(the volume in embedding space within which all words in the instance reside), while the mean summarizes the density per-dimension within the manifold (Hovy, 2015).

Second, we transform initial context-agnostic representations for target word types by taking an element-wise product of the word type vectors ( ~w∗) with vectors representing salient dimensions of the exemplar context (~c∗,max, ~c∗,min, ~c∗,mean) where ∗ ∈ {l, r} .
This yields three d-dimensional vectors for wl (~wl,max, ~wl,min, ~wl,mean), and three for wr (~wr,max, ~wr,min, ~wr,mean).

We refer to our final word-in-context representations for wl and wr as ~wl,mask and ~wr,mask respectively, where ~wl,mask is the concatenation of ~wl,max, ~wl,min, ~wl,mean, and ~wr,mask is also similarly constructed.
An alternative approach to contextualizing word representations is to directly compare the representations of words with representations of contexts.

originals: In a single language, this can be done using Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016), a neural model that, given a target word and its sentential context, embeds both the word and the context in the same low-dimensional space, with the objective of having the context predict the target word via a log linear model.
paraphrase_1: For monolingual applications, Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016) offers a solution through its neural architecture that maps both target words and their surrounding sentential contexts into a shared low-dimensional space, where the context serves to predict the target word through a log linear model.
paraphrase_2: Within a monolingual setting, one can employ Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016), which uses a neural framework to project target words alongside their sentential contexts into an identical low-dimensional representation space, designed so that contexts can predict target words via a log linear model.
paraphrase_3: Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016) provides a neural approach for single-language tasks by embedding target words and their sentential contexts within the same low-dimensional space, structured to enable context-based prediction of target words using a log linear model.
paraphrase_4: When working with one language, Context2Vec (Melamud et al., 2016) can be utilized as a neural solution that places both target words and their sentential contexts in a unified low-dimensional embedding space, where a log linear model allows contexts to predict the corresponding target words.

originals: Prior work on lexical entailment out of context suggests that the entailment relationship between words is a learnable function of the concatenation of their individual representations (Baroni et al., 2012; Turney and Mohammad, 2013).

We hypothesize that entailment relations hold between related words and introduce similarity features to capture this non-directional relation between words and contexts.

originals: Given a pair of vectors (~l, ~r), we use three similarity measures: the cosine similarity cosine(~l, ~r), the dot product ~l · ~r, and the euclidean distance ‖~l − ~r‖.

The cosine similarity captures the difference in the two vectors in terms of the angle between then, the euclidean distance measures the difference in magnitude, and the dot product captures both magnitude and angle.

originals: Our first set of similarities is intended to directly capture the similarities between contextualized word representations (Section 3.1).
We calculate pairwise similarities between (~wl,max, ~wr,max), (~wl,min, ~wr,min), (~wl,mean, ~wr,mean), as well as between the context-agnostic representations (~wl, ~wr).
Second, we add similarities based on Context2Vec representations, i.e.
between each pair of ~wl,c2v, ~wr,c2v, ~cl,c2v, and ~cr,c2v.
Finally, following Shwartz and Dagan (2015), we capture the similarity of the most relevant word to wl in cr, that to wr in cl, as well as between cl and cr.

originals: Modeling word meaning in context Several approaches have been proposed to model the meaning of a word in a given context to capture semantic equivalence in tasks such as lexical substitution, word sense disambiguation or paraphrase ranking (but not entailment).
paraphrase_1: Multiple methods have been developed for representing contextual word meanings, enabling the capture of semantic equivalence across applications like paraphrase ranking, lexical substitution, and word sense disambiguation (but not entailment).
paraphrase_2: Various techniques for contextual word meaning representation have been suggested, aimed at modeling semantic equivalence in areas including word sense disambiguation, paraphrase ranking, and lexical substitution (but not entailment).
paraphrase_3: To model how words convey meaning within specific contexts, researchers have introduced several approaches that address semantic equivalence challenges in lexical substitution, paraphrase ranking, and word sense disambiguation (but not entailment).
paraphrase_4: Different methodologies have emerged for capturing the contextual semantics of words, designed to handle semantic equivalence in tasks like word sense disambiguation, lexical substitution, and paraphrase ranking (but not entailment).

originals: One line of work (Dinu and Lapata, 2010; Reisinger and Mooney, 2010) treats each word as a set of latent word senses.
These models methods start with token representations for individual occurrences of a word and then choose a set of token vectors based on the current context.
paraphrase_1: Starting with token representations for individual word occurrences, these models methods select a set of token vectors according to the current context.
paraphrase_2: These models methods begin by using token representations for each word occurrence, then select token vectors based on what the current context requires.
paraphrase_3: Token representations for individual word occurrences serve as the starting point for these models methods, which subsequently choose token vectors depending on the current context.
paraphrase_4: Beginning with token representations that correspond to individual word occurrences, these models methods proceed to select token vectors according to the current context.

originals: An alternate set of models (Erk and Padó, 2008; Thater et al., 2011; Dinu et al., 2012) avoids defining a fixed set of word senses, and instead contextualizes word type vectors as we do in this paper.
These models share the idea of using an element-wise multiplication to apply a context mask to word type representations.
paraphrase_1: The concept of applying a context mask to word type representations through element-wise multiplication is common to these models.
paraphrase_2: An element-wise multiplication approach for applying context masks to word type representations is utilized across these models.
paraphrase_3: All these models employ the same principle: using element-wise multiplication as a method to apply context masks to word type representations.
paraphrase_4: The shared approach among these models involves element-wise multiplication for the application of context masks to word type representations.

originals: The nature of the context representation varies: Erk and Pado (2008) use inverse selectional preferences; Thater et al (2010) combine a first order representation for the context with a second order representation for the target, Thater et al.
(2011) rely on syntactic dependencies to define context.
Apidianaki (2016) shows that bag-ofword context representation within a small context window works as well as syntactic definitions of context for ranking paraphrases in context.
paraphrase_1: Ranking paraphrases in context proves equally effective whether using bag-ofword context representation within a small context window or syntactic definitions of context, according to Apidianaki (2016).
paraphrase_2: According to Apidianaki (2016), syntactic definitions of context perform no better than bag-ofword context representation within a small context window when it comes to ranking paraphrases in context.
paraphrase_3: Apidianaki (2016) demonstrates that for ranking paraphrases in context, bag-ofword context representation within a small context window achieves performance equivalent to that of syntactic definitions of context.
paraphrase_4: The research by Apidianaki (2016) reveals that bag-ofword context representation within a small context window matches the effectiveness of syntactic definitions of context in the task of ranking paraphrases in context.

originals: The use of convolution is motivated by success of similar models on sentence classification tasks.
paraphrase_1: Similar models' success on sentence classification tasks provides the motivation for using convolution.
paraphrase_2: Convolution is employed because analogous models have proven successful in sentence classification tasks.
paraphrase_3: The decision to apply convolution stems from the effectiveness of comparable models in sentence classification applications.
paraphrase_4: Success achieved by related models in sentence classification tasks drives the adoption of convolution.

originals: Tang et al (2014) uses convolution over embedding matrices for unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams, while Hovy (2015) uses just unigrams.

However, all these works only use convolved representations
to predict properties of the sentence (e.g., sentiment).
We use them, instead, to contextualize our target word representations.

In-context lexical semantic tasks Besides entailment, other lexical semantic tasks studied in the presence of context include lexical substitution (McCarthy and Navigli, 2007), cross-lingual lexical substitution (Mihalcea et al., 2010) and paraphrase ranking (Apidianaki, 2016).
The last work is also notable because of their successful use of models of word-meaning in context from Thater et al (2011), which is closely related to our methods.
We proposed to address lexical entailment in context, providing exemplar sentences to ground the meaning of words being considered for entailment.
paraphrase_1: Our approach involved tackling lexical entailment within contextual frameworks by supplying exemplar sentences that establish the semantic foundation for words under entailment consideration.
paraphrase_2: To handle lexical entailment in context, we suggested offering exemplar sentences that anchor the definitions of words being evaluated for entailment relationships.
paraphrase_3: The method we put forward focused on contextual lexical entailment, where exemplar sentences serve to establish the meanings of words targeted for entailment analysis.
paraphrase_4: We recommended confronting lexical entailment through contextual means, utilizing exemplar sentences to solidify the semantic understanding of words examined for entailment.

originals: We show that contextualized word representations constructed by transforming contextagnostic representations, combined with wordcontext similarity features, lead to large improvements over context-agnostic model, not only in English, but also between English and French words on two novel datasets.
paraphrase_1: Our findings demonstrate that contextualized word representations, created through the transformation of context-agnostic representations and enhanced with wordcontext similarity features, produce substantial performance gains compared to context-agnostic model across English as well as English-French word pairs in two new datasets.
paraphrase_2: The results reveal that when context-agnostic representations are transformed into contextualized word representations and augmented with wordcontext similarity features, significant improvements over context-agnostic model are achieved not just for English but also for English-French word combinations across two newly introduced datasets.
paraphrase_3: Through our research, we establish that contextualized word representations derived from transformed context-agnostic representations, when paired with wordcontext similarity features, yield considerable enhancements beyond context-agnostic model performance in both monolingual English and cross-lingual English-French scenarios on two original datasets.
paraphrase_4: Our work establishes that transforming context-agnostic representations into contextualized word representations, supplemented by wordcontext similarity features, results in marked improvements over context-agnostic model across both English monolingual and English-French bilingual word tasks using two newly created datasets.

originals: We also improve the state-of-the-art on a related task of detecting semantic relations in context.
Our features are sensitive to changes in entailment based on context, and also capture the directionality of entailment.
paraphrase_1: The directionality of entailment is captured by our features, which are also sensitive to context-based changes in entailment.
paraphrase_2: Context-driven variations in entailment are detected by our features, while they simultaneously record entailment directionality.
paraphrase_3: Both the directionality of entailment and sensitivity to contextual shifts in entailment are reflected in our features.
paraphrase_4: When entailment changes due to context, our features respond sensitively, and they additionally encode entailment directionality.

originals: In future work, we aim to further improve performance on both monolingual and cross-lingual datasets.
paraphrase_1: Our future research will focus on enhancing performance across monolingual and cross-lingual datasets alike.
paraphrase_2: We plan to advance the effectiveness of our approach on monolingual as well as cross-lingual datasets in subsequent studies.
paraphrase_3: Future research efforts will be directed toward boosting performance for both monolingual and cross-lingual datasets.
paraphrase_4: In upcoming investigations, we intend to enhance our system's effectiveness on monolingual and cross-lingual datasets equally.

originals: We anticipate that richer features, capturing second-order comparisons used to detect lexical contrast (Mohammad et al., 2013) and entailment out of context (Turney and Mohammad, 2013), might be useful for this purpose, perhaps in combination with non-linear classifiers.
Given the usefulness of similarities, we also plan to investigate asymmetric scoring functions(Kotlerman et al., 2010; Shwartz et al., 2017).
